main_actor: Athenian hoplite army with Plataean allies
description: An Athenian hoplite force led by Miltiades and Callimachus defeated a numerically superior Persian expeditionary force sent by Darius I. The victory preserved the independence of the Greek city-states and marked the failure of the First Persian invasion of Greece.
casualties_source: Herodotus, Histories 6.117 (Persian losses; Athenian losses given as 192)
ethical_notes: Casualty figures come primarily from Herodotus, writing a generation later; modern estimates vary. The Persian force size is particularly debated — Herodotus' 'numberless ships' is clearly rhetorical.

sources.citation: Herodotus, Histories, Book VI. (Loeb Classical Library edition, trans. A.D. Godley, 1922).
Krentz, P. (2010). The Battle of Marathon. Yale University Press.

main_actor: Multiple actors across centuries (Julius Caesar 48 BCE fire, Aurelian 273 CE, Theophilus 391 CE, Amr ibn al-As 642 CE)
description: The Library of Alexandria was progressively destroyed across six centuries through a series of distinct events: the fire during Caesar's siege of Alexandria in 48 BCE, Aurelian's suppression of the Brucheion in 273 CE, the Christian destruction of the Serapeum in 391 CE, and the Arab conquest in 642 CE. Ancient sources attribute the loss to one or another of these events depending on the author's agenda. The compound result was the irrecoverable loss of classical Mediterranean scholarship.
silence_reason: The scale, timing, and attribution of the library's destruction is itself contested because each political-religious tradition attributes the loss to its rivals (Christians blame pagans/Arabs, Muslims blame Christians, secular historians blame all three). No surviving contemporary account is neutral.
ethical_notes: Treating this as a single event is a simplification accepted here because the cumulative result — loss of classical scholarship — was historically real, even if the mechanism was multi-step and disputed. Use this event as a linking anchor; the detail work belongs in specialized scholarship.

sources.citation: El-Abbadi, M. (1990). The Life and Fate of the Ancient Library of Alexandria. UNESCO.
Plutarch, Life of Caesar 49 (primary attribution to the 48 BCE fire).

main_actor: Octavian (Gaius Julius Caesar Octavianus) and Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa
description: Octavian's fleet under Agrippa defeated the combined forces of Marcus Antonius and Cleopatra VII of Egypt. The victory ended the final war of the Roman Republic, eliminated Antony and Cleopatra as rivals, and led directly to the formal establishment of the Principate in 27 BCE.
casualties_source: Lange, C. (2011). Res Publica Constituta.

entity_links.notes: Actium is the practical founding moment of the Augustan Principate, though Octavian did not formally assume the title Augustus until 27 BCE.

sources.citation: Plutarch, Life of Antony. (Loeb Classical Library edition).
Gurval, R.A. (1995). Actium and Augustus: The Politics and Emotions of Civil War. University of Michigan Press.

main_actor: Emperor Caracalla (Marcus Aurelius Antoninus)
description: Following the murder of his brother and co-emperor Publius Septimius Geta by Caracalla on 26 December 211 CE, the Roman Senate ordered a formal damnatio memoriae. Geta's name was chiseled from inscriptions across the empire, his images were defaced on coins and the Arch of Septimius Severus, and reading or speaking his name became a capital offense. The edict is the most systematically documented state-level erasure from Roman antiquity.
casualties_source: Cassius Dio 77.4.1 (figure for Geta's supporters subsequently executed)
silence_reason: The event itself is well-documented because the erasure was so systematic that the absences are visible on physical monuments. But the intellectual content Geta may have produced, any writings by his supporters, and much of his reign's administrative record were permanently lost.
ethical_notes: Damnatio memoriae is included as an event type because the act of erasure is itself a historical fact with downstream epistemic consequences. Other Roman emperors subjected to it (Domitian, Commodus formally rescinded) are candidates for similar entries.

sources.citation: Cassius Dio, Roman History, Book 77.
Varner, E.R. (2004). Mutilation and Transformation: Damnatio Memoriae and Roman Imperial Portraiture. Brill.

location_name: Route from Mecca to Yathrib (Medina), Hejaz
main_actor: Muhammad ibn Abdullah and early Muslim community
description: Muhammad and his followers migrated from Mecca to Yathrib (renamed Medina) to escape persecution from the Quraysh. The migration established the first Muslim polity and marks Year 1 of the Islamic (Hijri) calendar — the formal start of the Islamic era for historiographical purposes.

sources.citation: Ibn Ishaq / Ibn Hisham, Sirat Rasul Allah (trans. A. Guillaume, 1955).
Watt, W.M. (1956). Muhammad at Medina. Oxford University Press.

main_actor: Seljuk Sultanate under Alp Arslan
description: Seljuk forces decisively defeated a Byzantine army led by Emperor Romanos IV Diogenes, who was captured on the battlefield. The defeat shattered the Byzantine theme system in Anatolia and opened the region to Turkic migration — the demographic shift that eventually produced modern Turkey.
casualties_source: Cahen, C. (1968). Pre-Ottoman Turkey.

sources.citation: Attaleiates, Michael. History. (CFHB edition).
Vryonis, S. (1971). The Decline of Medieval Hellenism in Asia Minor. University of California Press.

main_actor: Yersinia pestis (bubonic plague), carried along Silk Road and Mediterranean trade routes
description: A bubonic plague pandemic caused by Yersinia pestis, probably originating in Central Asia, reached Crimea in 1346 and Mediterranean ports in 1347. Within four years it killed an estimated 30-60% of Europe's population, with comparable mortality in the Middle East and North Africa. The demographic collapse restructured European labor markets, accelerated the decline of serfdom in Western Europe, and produced massive anti-Jewish pogroms driven by scapegoating.
casualties_source: Benedictow, O.J. (2004). The Black Death 1346-1353.
ethical_notes: Casualty range is genuinely large because regional demographic records are uneven. Central Asian and sub-Saharan mortality estimates are the least certain.

sources.citation: Benedictow, O.J. (2004). The Black Death 1346-1353: The Complete History. Boydell Press.
Green, M.H. (ed.) (2015). Pandemic Disease in the Medieval World. ARC Medieval Press.

main_actor: Ottoman Sultan Mehmed II Fatih
description: Ottoman forces under Mehmed II, using massive bombards cast by the Hungarian engineer Urban, breached the Theodosian Walls of Constantinople after a 53-day siege. Emperor Constantine XI Palaiologos died in the final assault. The fall ended the 1123-year continuity of the Roman state, triggered a westward flight of Greek scholars (a factor in the Italian Renaissance), and made Mehmed the effective ruler of the Eastern Mediterranean.
casualties_source: Runciman, S. (1965). The Fall of Constantinople 1453.
ethical_notes: Name and framing vary radically: 'Fall' (Western Christian), 'Fetih' / Conquest (Ottoman/Turkish), 'Ἅλωσις' / capture (Greek). All three names refer to the same event but carry opposite valences.

sources.citation: Kritoboulos of Imbros, History of Mehmed the Conqueror (trans. Riggs, 1954).
Runciman, S. (1965). The Fall of Constantinople 1453. Cambridge University Press.
Philippides, M. & Hanak, W. (2011). The Siege and the Fall of Constantinople in 1453. Ashgate.

main_actor: Spanish conquistadores under Hernán Cortés allied with Tlaxcalteca, Totonac, and other indigenous groups hostile to the Mexica
description: A Spanish expedition of roughly 500 men allied with tens of thousands of indigenous warriors — primarily from Tlaxcala, long at war with the Mexica — besieged and destroyed Tenōchtitlan, capital of the Aztec Triple Alliance. A smallpox epidemic, introduced unintentionally by the Spanish, killed a substantial fraction of the Mexica defenders during the siege. After the city's fall in August 1521, the Spanish systematically demolished temples and civic buildings and built Mexico City on the ruins.
casualties_source: Restall, M. (2003). Seven Myths of the Spanish Conquest (combat + siege smallpox).
ethical_notes: The framing as solely a 'Spanish conquest' is Eurocentric. The event was a multi-front indigenous war in which the Spanish were one of many actors — Tlaxcalteca casualties and choices are essential to the history.

sources.citation: Sahagún, B. de, Historia general de las cosas de Nueva España (Florentine Codex), Book 12.
Restall, M. (2003). Seven Myths of the Spanish Conquest. Oxford University Press.
Townsend, C. (2019). Fifth Sun: A New History of the Aztecs. Oxford University Press.

location_name: Osnabrück and Münster, Holy Roman Empire
main_actor: Ferdinand III (HRE), Louis XIV (France, via Mazarin), Christina (Sweden), United Provinces, Swiss Confederation, and 190+ other signatories
description: Two treaties signed at Osnabrück and Münster ended the Thirty Years War and the Eighty Years War. Beyond the immediate cessation of hostilities, the settlement established the principle of territorial sovereignty (often simplified as 'Westphalian sovereignty'), recognized the independence of the Dutch Republic and the Swiss Confederation, and granted religious parity to Calvinism alongside Catholicism and Lutheranism within the Empire.
ethical_notes: The 'Westphalian system' is often projected backward — modern IR theory exaggerated the immediate 1648 impact. Many of the attributed principles developed gradually across the 17th and 18th centuries.

sources.citation: Treaty of Osnabrück and Treaty of Münster, 1648 (Latin originals, Diplomatic Archive of the Treaty of Westphalia).
Wilson, P.H. (2009). The Thirty Years War: Europe's Tragedy. Belknap / Harvard.

location_name: Bengal Presidency, British East India Company territory
main_actor: British East India Company administration (post-Diwani rights 1765)
description: A famine in Bengal, exacerbated by East India Company revenue extraction that continued at full rates despite crop failure, killed an estimated one-third of the regional population. Company officials were explicitly instructed not to reduce tax collection; some officials engaged in grain speculation. The famine was a direct test case of the extractive colonial administration that replaced Mughal revenue systems.
casualties_source: Datta, R. (2000). Society, Economy and the Market: Commercialization in Rural Bengal.
ethical_notes: This famine is one of the earliest clearly documented cases of colonial-administration-induced mass starvation. The English-language historiography has at times minimized the Company's causal role; use 'FAMINE' here without the euphemism 'food crisis'.

entity_links.notes: Formally the East India Company, precursor to the Raj (established 1858).

sources.citation: Datta, R. (2000). Society, Economy and the Market: Commercialization in Rural Bengal, c. 1760-1800. Manohar.
Sen, A. (1981). Poverty and Famines: An Essay on Entitlement and Deprivation. Oxford University Press.

location_name: Mount Tambora, Sumbawa, Dutch East Indies
description: The largest volcanic eruption in recorded human history, with a VEI of 7. The eruption killed approximately 10,000 people directly through pyroclastic flows on Sumbawa, and an estimated 70,000-120,000 globally through the subsequent 'Year Without a Summer' (1816) — crop failures, famine, and epidemics in Europe, North America, and China.
casualties_source: Oppenheimer, C. (2003). Climatic, environmental and human consequences of the largest known historic eruption.

sources.citation: Oppenheimer, C. (2003). 'Climatic, environmental and human consequences of the largest known historic eruption: Tambora volcano (Indonesia) 1815.' Progress in Physical Geography 27(2).
Wood, G.D. (2014). Tambora: The Eruption That Changed the World. Princeton University Press.

name_original: Vernichtungsbefehl gegen die Herero und Nama
main_actor: Imperial German Army under General Lothar von Trotha (Vernichtungsbefehl, 2 October 1904)
description: After Herero uprisings against German colonial dispossession, General Lothar von Trotha issued an explicit extermination order (Vernichtungsbefehl) declaring that 'every Herero, armed or unarmed' found on German territory 'would be shot'. Survivors of direct killings were driven into the Omaheke desert, where most died of thirst; others were placed in concentration camps (Konzentrationslager — an early documented German use of the term) on Shark Island and elsewhere. An estimated 65-80% of the Herero population and 50% of the Nama population died.
casualties_source: Zimmerer, J. (2004) & Olusoga, D. (2010)
silence_reason: The German government officially denied genocide classification for 113 years, from 1908 until the May 2021 formal recognition. German-language archives of the period downplay or omit the scale of killings. Contemporary recognition depends on survivor testimony, Herero-Nama oral tradition, missionary records, and post-1960s German academic research — not on the official colonial archive.
ethical_notes: Germany formally recognized the genocide in May 2021 and agreed to pay 1.1 billion euros in development aid over 30 years. Recognition does NOT retroactively fix the century-long silence of official sources, nor does the 'aid' framing constitute legal reparations.

sources.citation: Zimmerer, J. (2004). 'The birth of the Ostland out of the spirit of colonialism.' Patterns of Prejudice 38(1).
Olusoga, D. & Erichsen, C.W. (2010). The Kaiser's Holocaust: Germany's Forgotten Genocide. Faber & Faber.
Oral testimony collected by UN Special Rapporteur on racism, 2015-2019.

location_name: Ottoman Empire (primarily Anatolia and Syrian deserts)
main_actor: Ottoman government under the Committee of Union and Progress (CUP / İttihat ve Terakki Cemiyeti)
description: The Ottoman government under the CUP ordered and organized the mass deportation and killing of Armenian civilians across the empire, beginning with the arrest of Armenian intellectuals in Constantinople on 24 April 1915. Victims were forced-marched into the Syrian desert, executed en route, or held in concentration camps. The events constitute the Armenian Genocide, recognized by most university historians, the International Association of Genocide Scholars, and most Western governments.
casualties_source: Akçam, T. (2012). The Young Turks' Crime against Humanity.
silence_reason: The Republic of Turkey officially denies the genocide classification and criminalizes (Article 301) descriptions calling it 'genocide'. The Ottoman-era archival record was selectively destroyed or remains inaccessible. Armenian documentation was lost with Armenian populations.
ethical_notes: The 'disputed' status here reflects the political controversy with the Turkish state, NOT the historical-academic consensus. The IAGS (1997), most US universities, and most Western governments classify the event as genocide. This field models the distinction: confidence_score 0.95 reflects academic certainty; status 'disputed' reflects the live political contestation.

sources.citation: Akçam, T. (2012). The Young Turks' Crime against Humanity: The Armenian Genocide and Ethnic Cleansing in the Ottoman Empire. Princeton University Press.
Suny, R.G. (2015). 'They Can Live in the Desert but Nowhere Else': A History of the Armenian Genocide. Princeton University Press.
IAGS Resolution on the Armenian Genocide, 1997.

location_name: Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic (primarily) and Kuban
main_actor: Soviet government under Joseph Stalin (grain requisition policy)
description: A famine in Ukraine caused by Soviet forced-collectivization grain requisition quotas applied through the 1932 harvest, combined with punitive blacklisting of villages and restrictions on peasant mobility. An estimated 3.5-7.5 million Ukrainians died. Ukraine and 15+ other countries classify the Holodomor as genocide; other scholars dispute the genocide classification while accepting the mass-starvation facts.
casualties_source: Snyder, T. (2010). Bloodlands.
silence_reason: The Soviet government officially denied the famine existed until the mid-1980s. Western journalists in Moscow (notably Walter Duranty, NYT) actively suppressed coverage in exchange for access. Soviet archival access became possible only after 1991; significant portions remain classified in Russian state archives.
ethical_notes: Ukraine classifies the event as genocide (Golodomor is Ukrainian for 'killing by hunger'). Russian official historiography disputes the genocide classification while acknowledging the famine. This entry uses event_type FAMINE because the mechanism (starvation from policy) is factual; the distinct question of 'genocide' vs 'policy-induced mass death' is genuinely debated among historians and is reflected in status=disputed.

sources.citation: Conquest, R. (1986). The Harvest of Sorrow: Soviet Collectivization and the Terror-Famine. Oxford University Press.
Snyder, T. (2010). Bloodlands: Europe Between Hitler and Stalin. Basic Books.
Ukrainian oral history collected by the Holodomor Research and Education Consortium (ongoing).

main_actor: Nazi German state (NSDAP / SS / Wehrmacht / Einsatzgruppen / collaborating regimes)
description: The systematic state-organized murder of approximately 6 million European Jews by Nazi Germany and collaborating regimes between 1941 and 1945. Additional victims of Nazi extermination policy included approximately 200,000-500,000 Roma (Porajmos), up to 500,000 disabled people (Aktion T4), homosexuals, Slavic civilians, and Jehovah's Witnesses. Killing methods included mass shootings (Einsatzgruppen), death camps (Auschwitz-Birkenau, Treblinka, Sobibor, Belzec, Chelmno, Majdanek), and systematic starvation in ghettos.
casualties_source: Hilberg, R. (1985). The Destruction of the European Jews, 3rd ed.
ethical_notes: Holocaust denial is illegal in many jurisdictions and is not an academic position. The name 'Shoah' (Hebrew: 'catastrophe') is preferred by many Jewish scholars over the etymologically-religious 'Holocaust' (Greek: 'burnt offering'). Both names are in wide academic use.

sources.citation: Hilberg, R. (1985). The Destruction of the European Jews, 3rd ed. Holmes & Meier.
Longerich, P. (2010). Holocaust: The Nazi Persecution and Murder of the Jews. Oxford University Press.
Yad Vashem archival documentation (ongoing).

name_original: Gkasaya ya Rwanda / Jenoside yakorewe Abatutsi
main_actor: Hutu Power government of Rwanda (MRND, CDR) and Interahamwe militia
description: Between 7 April and mid-July 1994, the Hutu-led government and Interahamwe militia killed approximately 500,000-1,000,000 Tutsi and moderate Hutu civilians. The killings, conducted largely with machetes and small arms at the community level, were coordinated through state radio (RTLM), road blocks, and prepared kill lists. The genocide ended when the Rwandan Patriotic Front (RPF) captured Kigali in July 1994.
casualties_source: Des Forges, A. (1999). Leave None to Tell the Story.
ethical_notes: The UN and many governments acknowledged the genocide as it was happening but did not intervene. This failure of international responsibility is itself part of the event's historical record. The RPF's post-1994 government has also been documented as committing war crimes during and after the genocide (Mapping Report, OHCHR 2010) — this is a separate set of events not covered here.

sources.citation: Des Forges, A. (1999). Leave None to Tell the Story: Genocide in Rwanda. Human Rights Watch.
Prunier, G. (1995). The Rwanda Crisis: History of a Genocide. Columbia University Press.
ICTR (International Criminal Tribunal for Rwanda) trial records, 1995-2015.

main_actor: UK Colonial Office and successor Foreign and Commonwealth Office
description: The UK Colonial Office systematically destroyed, repatriated, or sequestered sensitive documents from colonies approaching independence. Documents recording torture, detention, extrajudicial killings, and other abuses during independence movements (Kenya Mau Mau, Malaya Emergency, Cyprus EOKA, Aden, etc.) were burned, dumped at sea (with weighted sacks to ensure they sank), or hidden in a secret archive at Hanslope Park. The existence of the hidden archive was denied for decades and was confirmed only through a 2011 UK High Court case brought by Mau Mau survivors.
ethical_notes: Although this event itself is now well-documented (post-2011), it CAUSED the silencing of many other colonial-era events. Entities and events downstream of 1948-1966 British colonial administration carry partial-archive risk: their 'confidence_score' may be depressed not because the event is doubtful but because primary documentation was destroyed.

sources.citation: Elkins, C. (2005). Imperial Reckoning: The Untold Story of Britain's Gulag in Kenya. Henry Holt.
Anderson, D. (2011). Histories of the Hanged. W.W. Norton.
FCO Migrated Archives, partially released by UK High Court order 2011-2013.
Mau Mau veterans' testimony, collected 2003-2013.

main_actor: H1N1 influenza A virus; spread accelerated by WWI troop movements
description: An H1N1 influenza pandemic infected an estimated one-third of the global population and killed 17-100 million people worldwide across three waves (spring 1918, autumn 1918, early 1919). The 'Spanish flu' name reflects only that wartime censorship in belligerent countries suppressed reporting; Spain, being neutral, reported openly and was blamed by convention. Mortality was especially high among young adults — an unusual feature attributed to cytokine storm.
casualties_source: Johnson, N.P.A.S. & Mueller, J. (2002). Updating the Accounts.
ethical_notes: The wide casualty range reflects genuine uncertainty: wartime censorship, colonial under-reporting, and the 'ordinary flu death' coding problem mean national figures are unreliable. India alone lost an estimated 13-18 million.

sources.citation: Johnson, N.P.A.S. & Mueller, J. (2002). 'Updating the Accounts: Global Mortality of the 1918-1920 Spanish Influenza Pandemic.' Bulletin of the History of Medicine 76.
Barry, J.M. (2005). The Great Influenza. Penguin.

description: On the morning of 1 November 1755 (All Saints' Day), a magnitude ~8.5-9.0 earthquake destroyed most of Lisbon. A tsunami followed within minutes, and fires burned for five days. An estimated 30,000-100,000 died. The disaster provoked Europe-wide theological and philosophical crisis — Voltaire's 'Poème sur le désastre de Lisbonne' (1756) was a direct response — and accelerated the decline of optimistic theodicy.
casualties_source: Paice, E. (2008). Wrath of God: The Great Lisbon Earthquake of 1755.

sources.citation: Paice, E. (2008). Wrath of God: The Great Lisbon Earthquake of 1755. Quercus.
Voltaire (1756). Poème sur le désastre de Lisbonne.

name_original: 大津波 Jōgan 貞観地震
description: A magnitude ~8.6 earthquake off the Sanriku coast on 13 July 869 CE produced a massive tsunami that inundated the Sendai plain, killing approximately 1,000 people. Sediment deposits from the event were re-studied in the early 2000s; the findings warned that the Sendai area remained vulnerable to a similar-scale event, a warning that proved prescient for the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami.
casualties_source: Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku (primary record, c. 901 CE).

sources.citation: Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku (Japanese imperial chronicle, completed 901 CE).
Minoura, K. et al. (2001). 'The 869 Jōgan tsunami deposit and recurrence interval of large-scale tsunami on the Pacific coast of northeast Japan.' Journal of Natural Disaster Science 23(2).

calendar_note: Julian calendar; Gregorian equivalent may differ

name_original: Déclaration des Droits de l'Homme et du Citoyen
main_actor: National Constituent Assembly of France
description: The French National Constituent Assembly adopted the Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen on 26 August 1789. Its seventeen articles asserted natural rights of liberty, property, security, and resistance to oppression, and established the principle of equality before the law. The declaration became a foundational text of liberal constitutionalism worldwide — and a source of debate over the exclusions (women, enslaved people, colonial subjects) from its nominal 'universal' scope.
ethical_notes: The declaration's universal language is contradicted by its contemporaries' refusal to extend rights to enslaved people (explicitly excluded from the French colonies) and women (Olympe de Gouges' 1791 'Declaration of the Rights of Woman' was a direct protest). Include both the text's significance AND its exclusions.

sources.citation: Déclaration des Droits de l'Homme et du Citoyen (original text, 1789).
Hunt, L. (2007). Inventing Human Rights: A History. W.W. Norton.

main_actor: Enslaved African population of Saint-Domingue (Toussaint Louverture, Jean-Jacques Dessalines)
description: The only successful slave revolt in modern history. Enslaved Africans in Saint-Domingue, the wealthiest colony in the Caribbean, rose up on 22 August 1791. Over thirteen years they defeated French, British, and Spanish forces (including Napoleon's 1802 expedition) and declared independence on 1 January 1804 as Haiti — the first Black-led republic and the first in the Western Hemisphere to abolish slavery. France imposed an 'independence debt' of 150 million francs in 1825 (later reduced to 90 million), which Haiti paid in full by 1947 — a total extraction of over $21 billion in 2022 dollars.
casualties_source: Popkin, J. (2010). You Are All Free.
ethical_notes: The Haitian Revolution was systematically excluded from standard histories of 'the Age of Revolutions' for two centuries — a silence that itself is a fact about historiography. The post-1825 indemnity debt is a central cause of modern Haiti's economic underdevelopment and belongs in any history of the event.

sources.citation: James, C.L.R. (1938). The Black Jacobins: Toussaint L'Ouverture and the San Domingo Revolution. Secker & Warburg.
Popkin, J. (2010). You Are All Free: The Haitian Revolution and the Abolition of Slavery. Cambridge University Press.
Dubois, L. (2004). Avengers of the New World: The Story of the Haitian Revolution. Harvard University Press.

main_actor: German Chancellor Otto von Bismarck; 14 European powers + United States + Ottoman Empire
description: Fourteen European states, the United States, and the Ottoman Empire met in Berlin between November 1884 and February 1885 to regulate European colonization of Africa. No African representatives were present. The General Act formalized the 'effective occupation' principle, authorized King Leopold II's personal rule over the 'Congo Free State', and is retrospectively recognized as the legal framework for the Scramble for Africa — the 1880s-1910s colonization of ~90% of the continent.
ethical_notes: The absence of any African participant is the single most salient fact about this event. The polite diplomatic framing ('regulating colonization') is euphemistic; the outcome was the legal template for genocidal violence (Leopold's Congo) and dispossession of >200 million Africans.

sources.citation: General Act of the Berlin Conference, 26 February 1885 (original text).
Hochschild, A. (1998). King Leopold's Ghost. Houghton Mifflin.
Pakenham, T. (1991). The Scramble for Africa. Weidenfeld & Nicolson.

name_original: Partition of India / ਭਾਰਤ ਦੀ ਵੰਡ / भारत का विभाजन
main_actor: British Raj (drawing the Radcliffe Line) and political leadership of successor Dominions of India and Pakistan
description: On 14-15 August 1947, British India was partitioned into the Dominions of India and Pakistan along the Radcliffe Line, drawn by Cyril Radcliffe in five weeks with no prior knowledge of India. Approximately 14-18 million people were displaced (the largest mass migration in human history to that point). Inter-communal violence killed an estimated 500,000-2,000,000 Hindus, Muslims, and Sikhs. Women suffered mass sexual violence — an estimated 75,000-100,000 were abducted on both sides of the new border.
casualties_source: Talbot, I. & Singh, G. (2009). The Partition of India.
ethical_notes: The event belongs under ETHNIC_CLEANSING per academic consensus on bilateral population transfers under violence; some scholars (Brass, 2003) further argue for specific GENOCIDE classifications of local incidents. This entry defers to the widely-used ETHNIC_CLEANSING framing while acknowledging the debate. Archival silences remain severe — Radcliffe destroyed his working papers after completing the border.

sources.citation: Talbot, I. & Singh, G. (2009). The Partition of India. Cambridge University Press.
Butalia, U. (1998). The Other Side of Silence: Voices from the Partition of India. Penguin India.
Oral histories collected by the 1947 Partition Archive (ongoing).

location_name: Global (emergent in Wuhan, Hubei Province, China)
main_actor: SARS-CoV-2 virus (Sarbecovirus, Coronaviridae)
description: The COVID-19 pandemic, caused by SARS-CoV-2, was first detected in Wuhan in late 2019 and declared a pandemic by the WHO on 11 March 2020. Official confirmed global deaths reached approximately 7 million by 2024; excess-mortality estimates (accounting for undercounting) reached 18-28 million. The pandemic triggered the most extensive worldwide lockdowns in history, massive fiscal/monetary intervention, and accelerated structural shifts (remote work, e-commerce, mRNA vaccine platforms).
casualties_source: WHO Global Excess Deaths Estimates + Economist excess mortality model.
ethical_notes: The official confirmed-deaths figure (~7M) substantially undercounts true mortality due to testing gaps, capacity-exceeded hospitals, and deliberate under-reporting in some jurisdictions. Excess-mortality estimates (18-28M) are more accurate but less certain. The origin debate (natural spillover vs. lab leak) remains politically contested and empirically unresolved at the time of this record.

sources.citation: WHO Global Excess Deaths Associated with COVID-19 Report, 2022.
Wang, H. et al. (2022). 'Estimating excess mortality due to the COVID-19 pandemic.' The Lancet 399(10334).
Economist excess mortality tracker (ongoing).

main_actor: Johannes Gutenberg (with financial backing from Johann Fust)
description: Johannes Gutenberg developed a movable-type printing press using oil-based ink around 1450 in Mainz. The 42-line Bible (c. 1455) is the first major surviving printing produced with the system. The technology spread rapidly — by 1500 there were printing houses in 240+ European cities — and is a central driver of the 16th-century information transformation (Reformation, scientific revolution, rise of vernacular literature). Movable-type printing had been invented earlier in Korea (Jikji, 1377) and China (Bi Sheng, c. 1040), but Gutenberg's system is the ancestor of European mass printing.
ethical_notes: The 'Gutenberg as sole inventor' narrative is Eurocentric. Korean Jikji (1377) predates the Gutenberg Bible by ~80 years. The event is included here as a major technological inflection point for European historiography; a parallel entry for Jikji or Bi Sheng should be created to correct the imbalance.

sources.citation: Eisenstein, E. (1979). The Printing Press as an Agent of Change. Cambridge University Press.
Füssel, S. (2005). Gutenberg and the Impact of Printing. Ashgate.

name_original: The September Attacks / أحداث الحادي عشر من سبتمبر
main_actor: Al-Qaeda (19 hijackers, directed by Osama bin Laden and Khalid Sheikh Mohammed)
description: On 11 September 2001, 19 Al-Qaeda operatives hijacked four commercial aircraft in the United States. Two flew into the World Trade Center towers (New York), one into the Pentagon (Arlington), and the fourth crashed in Pennsylvania after passenger resistance. 2,977 civilian victims died; approximately 6,000 were injured. The attacks triggered the US-led 'War on Terror,' the invasion of Afghanistan (2001), the invasion of Iraq (2003, on pretexts subsequently proven false), and a global restructuring of surveillance and counterterrorism practice.
casualties_source: 9/11 Commission Report (2004).
ethical_notes: The downstream US response (Afghanistan 2001-2021, Iraq 2003-2011+, expanded surveillance regimes) killed hundreds of thousands of civilians across multiple countries; those events require separate entries and are not absorbed into this one. Framing the attacks as a turning point without accounting for the response is incomplete history.

sources.citation: National Commission on Terrorist Attacks Upon the United States (2004). The 9/11 Commission Report.
Wright, L. (2006). The Looming Tower: Al-Qaeda and the Road to 9/11. Alfred A. Knopf.

name_original: Fall of the Berlin Wall / Fall der Berliner Mauer
main_actor: East German citizens; SED/GDR government collapse; Günter Schabowski (accidental press conference)
description: On 9 November 1989, following months of Eastern Bloc unrest and a Hungarian border opening that had already drained the GDR of citizens, East German spokesman Günter Schabowski announced travel regulation changes at a press conference. When asked when the changes took effect, he said 'sofort, unverzüglich' ('immediately, without delay'). East Berliners converged on the Berlin Wall; overwhelmed border guards opened checkpoints rather than fire on the crowd. The Wall fell de facto that night and the GDR dissolved by October 1990.

sources.citation: Sarotte, M.E. (2014). The Collapse: The Accidental Opening of the Berlin Wall. Basic Books.
Mary Elise Sarotte interviews with Schabowski and border guards, 2009-2014 (oral history).

name_original: Discovery of the Americas by Columbus / Llegada de Colón
main_actor: Christopher Columbus (Cristoforo Colombo) acting for the Crown of Castile
description: On 12 October 1492, Christopher Columbus's expedition made landfall at Guanahani in the Bahamas, where the expedition encountered Taíno Lucayans. Columbus made three subsequent voyages. The contact initiated the Columbian Exchange — intercontinental transfer of plants, animals, diseases, and people between the Eastern and Western Hemispheres — and the Spanish colonization that killed 80-90% of the Taíno population within fifty years. The voyage is the starting point of the early-modern European colonial expansion and is inseparable from the genocidal demographic collapse that followed.
silence_reason: Indigenous naming (e.g., Guanahani) and Taíno cosmological accounts of 1492-1550 were systematically displaced by the Columbus-centric narrative preserved in Spanish sources. Taíno languages and ritual records were suppressed and largely lost by c. 1550; reconstruction depends on later ethnography, archaeology, and the few Spanish sources sympathetic enough to record indigenous accounts (Las Casas).
ethical_notes: The word 'discovery' (descubrimiento) is the contested Spanish-tradition term. Indigenous and decolonial historians use 'invasion', 'contact', 'encounter', or refuse a single label. This entry uses the Spanish-language title for searchability but the description makes the framing explicit. Columbus himself carried out documented atrocities (forced gold quotas, enslavement) in Hispaniola; the record is in his own lettersand Bobadilla's 1500 investigation.

sources.citation: Columbus, C. Diario del Primer Viaje (1492-1493, as abstracted by Las Casas).
Las Casas, B. de (c. 1550). Historia de las Indias.
Restall, M. (2003). Seven Myths of the Spanish Conquest. Oxford University Press.
Cook, N.D. (1998). Born to Die: Disease and New World Conquest. Cambridge University Press.

calendar_note: Julian calendar; Gregorian equivalent may differ

location_name: Nanjing (then capital of the Republic of China)
main_actor: Imperial Japanese Army (Central China Area Army, commanded by Prince Yasuhiko Asaka and General Iwane Matsui)
description: After capturing Nanjing on 13 December 1937, Imperial Japanese Army troops killed an estimated 100,000-300,000 Chinese civilians and disarmed combatants over six weeks. Documented methods included mass executions, bayonet contests, and systematic sexual violence against an estimated 20,000-80,000 women. The massacre is documented in diaries of German businessman John Rabe (who led the Nanking Safety Zone), missionary Minnie Vautrin, and surviving Chinese witnesses. The International Military Tribunal for the Far East (1948) convicted General Matsui of war crimes.
casualties_source: Chang, I. (1997). The Rape of Nanking; Wakabayashi, B.T. (ed.) (2007).
silence_reason: Japanese nationalist historians and some officials dispute the scale of the massacre and, in extreme cases, its occurrence. Japanese government archives were selectively destroyed in August 1945 before the US occupation; survivors' testimonies and contemporary documentation by third-country witnesses (Rabe, Vautrin, Magee) are therefore disproportionately important. The PRC maintains the 300,000 figure; academic consensus accepts the 100,000-300,000 range.
ethical_notes: 'Disputed' here reflects the Japanese-nationalist denial position, not academic consensus. IMTFE (1948) conviction of Matsui for war crimes is a legal finding of fact.

sources.citation: Chang, I. (1997). The Rape of Nanking: The Forgotten Holocaust of World War II. Basic Books.
Rabe, J. (ed. Wickert, 1997). The Good Man of Nanking: The Diaries of John Rabe.
Wakabayashi, B.T. (ed.) (2007). The Nanking Atrocity, 1937-38: Complicating the Picture. Berghahn Books.
International Military Tribunal for the Far East, Judgment (1948).
